figures.caption: Fig. 1. Overview of the proposed two-stage tactile-guided assembly framework. (a) Reach Stage: Vision-based imitation learning for autonomous grasping and approach. (b) Insert Stage: Tactile-rich reinforcement learning for fine-grained alignment and insertion under tight tolerances (0.05mm). The method achieves high success rates across diverse geometries while effectively suppressing interaction forces.
TABLE I SUCCESS RATES OF DIFFERENT METHODS ACROSS VARYING HOLE GEOMETRIES AND ASSEMBLY CLEARANCES.

Fig. 6. Comparison of human intervention rates during training for the 0.05 mm clearance task. The solid lines represent the mean values, while the shaded regions denote the standard deviation across multiple independent runs.

Fig. 9. Interaction force and torque comparison results between vanilla IL+RL and the proposed method during real-world 0.05 mm insertion. Vanilla policies exhibit high-frequency, high-amplitude force spikes, with peak normal force fluctuations exceeding 50 N and torque variations surpassing 160 N·mm. In contrast, the proposed method regulates interaction forces within a safe range, limiting the maximum normal force fluctuation to 20 N and torque variation to 90 N·mm.

Fig. 3. Overview of the proposed two-stage framework for peg-in-hole insertion. In the Reach stage, a diffusion policy generates short-horizon action sequences to move the peg near the hole entrance. In the Insert stage, Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) learns contact-rich insertion behaviors and recovery strategies. Human-in-the-loop intervention enables continuous collection of high-value interaction data, accelerating policy convergence.
Fig. 4. Illustration of the tactile baseline estimation and the resulting tactile change signals across different grasp poses.
